XPS measurements of the Ge 3d core level were performed at 90° photoelectron takeoff angles for samples with a 1 nm ALD-grown Al₂O₃ capping layer on Ge:H (black), GeOₓ (3 Å)/Ge (red), and GeOₓ (5 Å)/Ge (blue) surfaces. The chemical shifts corresponding to different Ge oxidation states are indicated below the spectra. The inset  shows the numerical deconvolution, expressed as percentages of the oxidation-state peak areas normalized to the Ge⁰ (3d₃/₂ + 3d₅/₂) peak area at 90°. The fitting procedure follows Ref. 52 and employs a Tougaard background. The binding energy separation between the Ge 3d₃/₂ and Ge 3d₅/₂ peaks is 0.58 eV, while the core-level shifts for Ge⁺¹, Ge⁺², Ge⁺³, and Ge⁺⁴ oxidation states are 0.8, 1.8, 2.7, and 3.3 eV, respectively, relative to the Ge 3d₅/₂ peak.

XPS measurements of the Ge 3d core level were performed at  15° photoelectron takeoff angles for samples with a 1 nm ALD-grown Al₂O₃ capping layer on Ge:H (black), GeOₓ (3 Å)/Ge (red), and GeOₓ (5 Å)/Ge (blue) surfaces. The chemical shifts corresponding to different Ge oxidation states are indicated below the spectra.

The graph represents the energy profile obtained from ReaxFF-NEB calculations for Al₂O₃ ALD on an H-terminated Ge(100) surface, showing the adsorption of trimethylaluminum (TMA). The reaction proceeds via chemisorption of TMA on a Ge–H* site, forming a Ge–Al(CH₃)₂* surface complex with the release of a CH₄ molecule, with an activation barrier of about 1.41 eV and an overall exothermic reaction energy of −1.09 eV. The insets illustrate the atomic configurations at each reaction stage.

The graph represents the energy profile obtained from ReaxFF-NEB calculations for the H₂O half-cycle of Al₂O₃ ALD on an H-terminated Ge(100) surface. It shows the reaction of H₂O with a Ge–H* site to form a Ge–OH* surface species and molecular H₂, with an activation energy of approximately 2.32 eV and a final reaction energy of about 1.04 eV. The insets depict the corresponding molecular adsorption and reaction configurations along the reaction pathway.

The graph represents the energy profile obtained from ReaxFF-NEB calculations for TMA adsorption on a Ge dangling bond (Ge–) site on the Ge(100) surface. TMA initially adsorbs strongly on the Ge– site, followed by a hydrogen-assisted ligand elimination reaction involving a neighboring Ge–H* site, resulting in the formation of a Ge–Al(CH₃)₂* complex and CH₄, with an overall reaction energy of −1.74 eV. The insets show the atomic arrangements corresponding to each intermediate and transition state.

This figure shows ReaxFF-NEB energy profiles for sequential surface reactions during Al₂O₃ ALD nucleation on Ge: This energy profile describes the first water exposure during Al₂O₃ ALD nucleation on a Ge surface terminated with Ge–Al(CH₃)₂*. H₂O initially adsorbs exothermically, followed by proton transfer and methyl elimination, forming a Ge–Al(CH₃)(OH)* intermediate with CH₄ release. The reaction proceeds with a low barrier and an overall negative energy change, indicating a thermodynamically favorable hydroxylation of the surface-bound Al species.

The second H₂O half-reaction further converts the remaining Al–CH₃ group into an Al–OH group through adsorption and ligand exchange. This step forms a fully hydroxylated Ge–Al(OH)₂* surface species with additional CH₄ formation. The energy profile shows modest activation barriers and an overall exothermic reaction, confirming that complete hydroxylation of the Al center is energetically favorable

In the following TMA exposure, Al(CH₃)₃ reacts with the Ge–Al(OH)₂* surface, forming an Al–O–Al bridged dimer via condensation and CH₄ elimination. This step is strongly exothermic, indicating stable Al–O–Al bond formation and effective nucleation of the Al₂O₃ network. The reaction establishes the foundation for continued layer-by-layer Al₂O₃ growth on the Ge substrate.

The figure displays calculated potential energy profiles (ReaxFF-NEB) for Al₂O₃ ALD surface reactions on Ge(100). Panel (a) shows the reaction of trimethylaluminum (TMA) with a passivated H-terminated surface, exhibiting an energy barrier of 1.41 eV.  (not quantitative)
Panel (b) shows the thermodynamically unfavorable reaction of water (H₂O) on the same surface. (not quantitative)

Panel (c) contrasts this behavior with the reaction on a Ge dangling-bond defect, which proceeds via barrier-less, spontaneous adsorption followed by ligand elimination.The line chart illustrates the percentage of oxygen diffusion over time for two different systems: GeOx/Ge and [Al2O3/GeOx]/Ge.

This line chart shows the stepwise increase in film thickness over time during Al₂O₃ Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) using TMA and H₂O on a GeOₓ/Ge substrate, with each thickness jump corresponding to one ALD cycle.
This multi axis chart shows the TMA dose peak and growth per cycle over ALD cycles. The values seems to dip initally till 3 ALD cycles and increased slowly.

This  line chart shows the evolution of Growth Per Cycle (GPC) for TMA adsorption over the first 15 ALD cycles, starting from a baseline. The GPC increases from an initial low value before stabilizing, indicating a nucleation period before steady-state growth is achieved.

Figure 3 presents a process flow diagram of the multiscale simulation architecture, showing how the mesoscopic model (ab initio methods and kinetic Monte Carlo) and the macroscopic CFD model are conjoined via a Linux Shell script interface. The diagram highlights the iterative exchange of pressure, temperature, and source generation/consumption between the models, enabling simultaneous simulation of surface kinetics and macroscopic fluid dynamics.

Conceptual diagram of the kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulation on a lattice, showing probabilistic assignment of reactions to 90,000 substrate sites based on weighted reaction rate constants. Time progression is determined by a secondary random number, and steric hindrance effects illustrate how bulky adsorbates affect neighboring site availability in the simulation.

The figure shows nitrogen mole fraction contours on a rotating wafer in reactor R2 for three gap distances (10 mm, 5 mm, and 1 mm) under steady-state and increasing angular velocities. The contours illustrate how nitrogen penetrates the reaction zones and how reagents are dragged into the purge zones as rotation speed increases. Smaller gap distances better suppress intermixing, while higher angular velocities promote nitrogen invasion and reagent runoff, defining operational limits for maintaining self-limiting behavior.

Hacac surface coverage (%) over time during adsorption (0.1 mole fraction), reaching full coverage at ~3.77 s. The progression reflects non-linear adsorption behavior due to integration of macroscopic fluid dynamics with mesoscopic surface kinetics.

BDEAS surface coverage (%) over time during adsorption (0.5 mole fraction), reaching full coverage at ~6.99 s. Slower adsorption is influenced by pressure depletion zones and reagent interactions in the fluid phase.

O₃ surface coverage (%) over time during adsorption (0.1 mole fraction), reaching full coverage at ~3.72 s. The curves indicate non-linear adsorption behavior and are consistent with experimental dosage times reported in the literature.

Figure 7 shows a line chart of Hacac partial pressure at a mesh node over time as a wafer rotates. The pressure rises around 0.8 s, reaches a plateau, and falls to zero around 3.6 s. The red line segment highlights the exposure time, representing the duration a wafer section is exposed to the reactant. This figure demonstrates how wafer rotation and reaction zone geometry determine exposure time distribution, which is crucial for achieving uniform surface coverage.

Figure 12 illustrates the concentration gradient of N₂ mole fraction along the reagent-purge boundary in reactor R4. The heatmap shows the small micrometer-scale variations in N₂ concentration, highlighting the limited spatial resolution available for capturing the temporal progression of surface coverage. These small length scales make it challenging to collect surface coverage data at high angular velocities and short time scales, which contributes to the discontinuous curves observed in Figure 11. The figure emphasizes the spatial difficulty in simulating adsorption dynamics near the boundary region.

A schematic of a sheet-to-sheet spatial atomic layer deposition reactor, showing separate reaction zones for different reagents and a conveyor belt transporting plate-shaped substrates to enable continuous, high-throughput deposition without lengthy purging steps.

Figure 2 presents a conceptual diagram of a multiscale modeling framework that connects mesoscopic simulations (ab initio quantum, statistical mechanics, kinetic Monte Carlo) with macroscopic simulations (reactor modeling and computational fluid dynamics). Initial state of the cross-linking reaction showing two adjacent SMIs within the A.1-O:13/56/2/4 blocking layer configuration (3.4 SMI/nm²). The two SMIs each bear a hydroxyl group (formed from methoxy hydrolysis during water pulse) positioned to undergo a condensation reaction.

Final state of the cross-linking reaction where the two SMIs are connected via an Si-O-Si bridge with release of water. The reaction has a moderate activation barrier (ΔE_A = 106 kJ/mol) and is slightly endothermic (ΔE_R = 7 kJ/mol). Values in parentheses show dispersion contributions.
Initial state showing TEA (green aluminum atom with ethyl ligands) adsorbed at the cross-linking oxygen that connects two SMIs within the blocking layer.

Final state after ligand exchange reaction where TEA has broken the cross-linking Si-O bond. The reaction has an activation barrier of 127 kJ/mol and is nearly thermoneutral (ΔE_R = -1 kJ/mol). The negative dispersion contributions indicate the product is destabilized relative to the reactant.

DB-O:13 conformer on A.1 where the unreacted methoxy group (blue circle) is oriented directly above the O2 silanol. This positioning allows the third condensation to form TB-O:123. The adsorption energy is -44 kJ/mol with -22 kJ/mol dispersion contribution. This conformer is 15 kJ/mol more stable than the one in (b).

Alternative DB-O:13 conformer where the propyl tail group (red circle) points toward O2, sterically blocking access. This orientation prevents both TB formation and adsorption of additional TMPS molecules at O2, leaving it as a potential nucleation site for Al precursors.

Precursor state showing MTMS (a monofunctional alkoxysilane) adsorbed at the free O2 silanol within the A.1-O:13/56/4 blocking layer configuration. The adsorption energy is -22 kJ/mol, dominated by dispersion (-113 kJ/mol), indicating the electronic contribution is actually unfavorable (+91 kJ/mol).

Product state after MTMS condenses with the O2 silanol, blocking the previously exposed site. The reaction has a high activation barrier (ΔE_A = 151 kJ/mol) and is slightly endothermic (ΔE_R = 6 kJ/mol). Values in parentheses show dispersion contributions.

The SMI treatment step shows the TMPS inhibitor structure (propyl blocking group, methoxy reactive groups) and its selective binding to silanol groups on the non-growth surface (a-SiO₂) while not affecting the growth surface (Cu). The red X indicates the SMI does not bind to Cu.
The ideal AS-ALD outcome where Al₂O₃ deposits exclusively on Cu(111) while the intact SMI layer completely protects the silica surface from deposition.
The realistic AS-ALD scenario where the SMI blocking layer progressively degrades, allowing Al₂O₃ nucleation on the NGS. Actual selectivity is determined by the ratio of nucleation between surfaces.

Top view of the SMI Layer Model (SLM) used for pEDA calculations to quantify inter-SMI interactions. The central SMI at position O2 (marked by blue ellipse) is selected as the fragment, and the double-pointed arrows indicate its interactions with neighboring SMIs in the layer. Each SMI shows the characteristic structure with a silicon atom (blue), oxygen atoms (red), carbon atoms (gray), and hydrogen atoms (white). The surface slab has been removed to create the SLM for analyzing SMI-SMI interactions independent of surface effects.

The chart shows activation energies (ΔE_A) for the C-O bond intersection reaction of TMA and TEA at three SMI layer densities. Barriers increase with SMI density: TMA rises from 179 to 207 kJ/mol, TEA from 160 to 197 kJ/mol. All barriers exceed 150 kJ/mol, indicating this reaction is unlikely under typical ALD conditions.

The chart shows activation energies for the methoxy-SMI intersection reaction with TMA, TEA, and H₂O. TMA and TEA barriers increase substantially with SMI density (99 to 173 kJ/mol for TMA; 115 to 203 kJ/mol for TEA), while H₂O barriers remain low (97 to 112 kJ/mol) regardless of SMI density.
Top view of the silanol-rich amorphous silica model (A.1) containing six surface silanols (labeled 1–6) at a density of 5.07 silanols per nm².
Top view of the silanol-reduced amorphous silica model (A.2) containing four surface silanols (labeled 2, 4, 5, 6) at a density of 3.38 silanols per nm², plus a reactive bridge oxygen (RBO) species.
Top view of the idealized α-quartz model (Q.1) containing only two distinguishable geminal silanol species (labeled 1, 2) at a much higher density of 9.34 silanols per nm².

Side view of the A.2-O:245 configuration showing a triply bonded (TB) TMPS molecule attached to silanol positions O2, O4, and O5 on the A.2 amorphous silica surface. The free silanol at O6 and the reactive bridge oxygen (RBO) species are labeled. The TB configuration requires significant framework distortion due to the large inter-silanol distances on A.2.

Side view of the A.1-O:123/56/4 full coverage configuration on the A.1 surface. This shows three SMIs: a TB-TMPS at positions O1, O2, O3; a DB-TMPS at positions O5, O6; and an SB-TMPS at position O4. All six surface silanols are blocked by this arrangement.
Top view of the A.2-O:245 configuration, showing the spatial arrangement of the TB-TMPS bonding to three silanols (O2, O4, O5), the free silanol O6, and the RBO beneath the blocking layer.
Top view of the A.1-O:123/56/4 configuration, illustrating how three SMIs achieve full silanol coverage on the A.1 surface. The numbered positions show the silanol bonding sites for each SMI.

This subfigure shows the formation of a triply bonded (TB) TMPS configuration on amorphous silica. Three surface silanol groups react with TMPS, releasing three methanol molecules and forming a TB SMI with Si−O linkages. The right step shows conceptual removal of the SMI (−SiPr) and hydrogen saturation (+3 H•) to generate the "TB residue" structure for energy calculations.

This subfigure illustrates how the TB bonding penalty (ΔE_TB-pen) is calculated: the energy difference between the "Relaxed Model" (pristine optimized surface) and the "TB Residue" (surface geometry after TB SMI removal). This quantifies the strain introduced into the silica framework by TB bonding.
The left column (a-d) shows reactions with Al precursors (TMA or TEA, where X = Me or Et). Starting with (a), representing RBO opening by ligand exchange
Si_SMI−O_surf bond disintegration
C−O bond intersection at methoxy groups
methoxy−Si_SMI intersection producing aluminum alkoxide.
The right column (e-g) shows hydrolysis reactions with water. Starting with (e), representing RBO hydrolysis to form silanols
Si_SMI−O_surf hydrolysis
methoxy hydrolysis.

Initial precursor state (PS) showing TMPS adsorbed near the reactive bridge oxygen (RBO) on the A.2 amorphous silica surface. The adsorption is strongly exothermic (ΔE_ads = -100 kJ/mol), with dispersion interactions contributing -65 kJ/mol. The RBO is the strained Si-O-Si bridge visible in the surface framework.

Final state after the RBO opening reaction, showing two products: a singly bonded (SB) TMPS and a methoxy group directly attached to a surface silicon atom. The reaction has a very low activation barrier (ΔE_A = 26 kJ/mol, with zero dispersion contribution) and is strongly exothermic (ΔE_R = -127 kJ/mol).

Conceptual diagram defining the distance metrics for analyzing SMI bonding configurations. d_init is the initial inter-silanol distance on the free surface, d_bond is the distance between surface oxygen atoms after forming a doubly bonded (DB) SMI, and Δd is their absolute difference representing the structural distortion required for bonding.

Scatter plot showing electronic energy (ΔE_elec) versus Δd for DB SMI configurations, referenced to the most stable A.1-O:13 configuration. Below Δd = 1.2 Å, energies remain moderate (~50 kJ/mol). Beyond this threshold, ΔE_elec increases dramatically (>100 kJ/mol), indicating large energy penalties for DB formation when significant silanol rearrangement is required.

Scatter plot showing ΔE_elec versus d_bond for SB/SB configurations (two singly bonded SMIs sharing adjacent silanols), referenced to geminal A.1-O:34 (empty circle). Electronic stability increases (more negative ΔE_elec) with larger d_bond, indicating that greater separation between two SB SMIs reduces steric repulsion.

Scatter plot showing ΔE_elec versus Δd for SB/SB configurations. Larger Δd correlates with less negative ΔE_elec, showing that surface distortion to accommodate two adjacent SMIs imposes an energy penalty that partially offsets the stabilization from increased separation.
Transition state geometry for RBO opening reaction with TEA, showing the Al precursor attacking the strained Si−O−Si bridge oxygen.
Transition state geometry for Si_SMI−O_surf bond disintegration, showing TEA attacking the bond connecting the SMI silicon to the surface oxygen, with a pentavalent Si_SMI intermediate.
Transition state geometry for C−O bond intersection at an unreacted methoxy group, where the methyl group transfers from oxygen to the TEA ethyl ligand.
Transition state geometry for methoxy−Si_SMI intersection, showing TEA approaching the Si_SMI center to form a pentavalent transition state while displacing the methoxy group.

The multiple line chart represents the Specific heat capacity (Cv) against temperature for various iron nanoparticles (FNP) with the dimensions (2 nm, 2.5 nm, 3.0 nm, 3.5 nm, and 4.0 nm). Sharp peaks were observed at specific temperatures indicating melting points of the respective particles. This graph illustrates the melting point depression phenomenon, where the melting temperature drops significantly (from 2100 K to 1525 K) as the nanoparticle diameter decreases from 4.0 nm to 2.0 nm. It confirms that smaller particles are thermodynamically less stable and undergo phase transitions at lower thermal energies compared to larger ones.

The diagram represent the Density Functional Theory (DFT)-calculated reaction energy values comparing the chemisorption of Hacac on Al₂O₃ surface. The diagrams show the activation energy (Eₐ) and reaction energy (ΔE) for the ligand-exchange reaction where Hacac replaces a surface hydroxyl group.

The key difference is that the reaction is exothermic and low-barrier on Al₂O₃ (ΔE = -0.49 eV, Eₐ = 0.01/0.25 eV) but endothermic and high-barrier on SiO₂ (ΔE = +0.98 eV, Eₐ = 2.35 eV).

Ligand exchange with one surface OH group. One methyl is released as methane (not shown), producing a surface-bound AlMe₂ species attached through a single Al–O bond.
Ligand exchange with two OH groups. Two methyls are released, yielding an AlMe species bridging two surface oxygen atoms.
Ligand exchange with three OH groups. All three methyls are released, leaving aluminum bonded to three surface oxygens with no remaining ligands.
Dissociation on an oxygen bridge. AlMe₃ splits across a surface O bridge (no OH required), depositing AlMe₂ bonded to one oxygen and a separate methyl group on an adjacent site.
Association with an oxygen bridge. AlMe₃ coordinates to a surface oxygen via a dative bond (dotted line) without dissociating, retaining all three methyl ligands.

The scatter plot shows the amount of aluminum adsorbed (Δc_Al) as a function of AlMe₃ reaction temperature on alumina pretreated at 560°C. The fitted line (y = a + bx) yields a = 2.9 ± 0.1 nm⁻² and b = −0.0003 ± 0.0005, indicating that Δc_Al remains essentially constant (~2.9 nm⁻²) across the 80–300°C range, demonstrating temperature independence of aluminum adsorption in this regime.

The scatter plot shows Δc_Al (aluminum adsorbed per cycle) versus surface OH concentration c_(O)H, combining data from alumina (triangles, 80–300°C) and silica (circles 150°C, squares 250°C). The solid line is a linear fit yielding a = 1.68 ± 0.09 nm⁻² and b = 0.37 ± 0.02. The dashed line represents the theoretical prediction assuming one OH binds one Al atom (Eq. 6), which clearly fails to describe the data.

The scatter plot shows Δc_Me (methyl groups adsorbed) versus c_(O)H. The solid fit gives a = 4.9 ± 0.2 nm⁻² and b = 0.14 ± 0.06, indicating Δc_Me is nearly constant (~5 nm⁻²) regardless of OH concentration. The dashed theoretical line again fails to match observations.

The scatter plot shows the average Me/Al ratio (Δc_Me/Δc_Al) versus c_(O)H. The ratio decreases from ~3 at low c_(O)H toward ~2 at high c_(O)H. The solid curve fits the experimental trend while the dashed line shows the inadequate theoretical prediction.

The scatter plot shows the effect of alumina heat-treatment temperature on both the aluminum adsorbed per cycle (Δc_Al, triangles) and the surface OH group concentration (c_(O)H, X markers). Both quantities decrease with increasing pretreatment temperature (200–800°C). The linear fit to Δc_Al yields a = 5.9 ± 0.2 nm⁻² and b = −0.0050 ± 0.0003, indicating that higher pretreatment temperatures reduce aluminum uptake.

The scatter plot shows analogous data for silica substrates, with Δc_Al measured at two reaction temperatures (150°C: circles; 250°C: squares) and c_(O)H shown as + markers. The fit gives a = 4.7 ± 0.3 nm⁻² and b = −0.0033 ± 0.0005. Both Δc_Al and c_(O)H decrease with heat-treatment temperature, with c_(O)H declining more steeply at higher temperatures.

The chart represents the QCM data showing TiO₂ mass changes during ALD and subsequent WF₆ doses at 120°C and 220°C; only the 220°C sample exhibits noticeable mass loss per dose, indicating active etching.
Magnified view of the WF₆ dosing period; 220°C shows stepwise mass loss (etching), while 120°C shows negligible change, confirming the reaction is thermally activated.

This figure presents high-resolution X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) scans tracking surface chemistry changes of a ~6.5 nm TiO₂ film during WF₆ exposure at 220°C. Ti 2p Region: The persistent Ti–O doublet confirms the presence of the TiO₂ film throughout the process. The intensity changes indicate partial modification or thinning of the oxide during etching.

Si 2p Region: Initially (0 Doses, black line), the signal is negligible. As WF₆ doses increase (red → pink), distinct Si–Si (substrate) and Si–O (interface) peaks appear, indicating thinning of the TiO₂ film and exposure of the underlying silicon wafer.
Tungsten Residue (W 4f Region): Shows accumulation of tungsten-containing species, overlapping with Ti–O signals, indicating incorporation or adsorption of WF₆ reaction products on the surface.
Fluorine Residue (F 1s Region): Tracks the buildup of fluorine on the surface (F–M and F–C), confirming surface fluorination during WF₆ exposure.

This figure presents high-resolution XPS spectra tracking the surface chemistry evolution of a TiO₂ film after 0, 15, and 25 cycles of a WF₆/Ar/Sn(acac)₂/Ar process at 220°C. The Titanium signal (Ti 2p) remains strong even after 25 cycles, showing that the TiO₂ film is largely preserved, though partial conversion of Ti–O to Ti–F occurs.
The Fluorine signal (F 1s) grows progressively, indicating fluorine incorporation on the surface.
The Tungsten signal (W 4f) shows significant accumulation, reflecting the formation of a tungsten oxyfluoride layer (W–O/F).

The Tin signal (Sn 3d) also increases, showing the appearance of tin oxide (Sn–O) species and contributing to the formation of a mixed metal-fluoride/oxide layer (W–Sn–F–O) atop the TiO₂. The data reveals that, unlike the etching behavior seen with BCl₃, this reactant combination does not remove the TiO₂ film but instead leads to surface modification and deposition of additional species

The figure characterizes the saturation behavior of a thermal Atomic Layer Etching (ALE) process for Titanium Dioxide , Etch rate as a function of BCl₃ dose time at 170 °C, showing clear saturation and self-limiting behavior characteristic of thermal ALE.
Etch rate as a function of WF₆ dose time at 170 °C, also exhibiting saturation, confirming that the TiO₂ etching process follows an ALE mechanism rather than continuous etching.

QCM data showing TiO₂ etching for different thicknesses (50, 100, 200 ALD cycles) at 220 °C. Thin films etch immediately, while thick films show an incubation period (mass gain/plateau) before etching due to crystallization increasing chemical resistance.
This mutliple line chart focusing on the mass loading of TiO₂ cycles and WF₆ doses over time, highlighting the effect of 50 doses at specific intervals.

This figure uses Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) data to demonstrate the selective etching of Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂) over Aluminum Oxide (Al₂O₃) at 170 °C. The image shows the full process timeline. Both materials are first deposited via ALD (staircase growth). Upon switching to the ALE recipe (WF₆/BCl₃), TiO₂ (red line) immediately begins to lose mass linearly (etching), while Al₂O₃ (black line) gains a small amount of mass and then remains stable (resistant).

A magnified view of the first few ALE cycles. TiO₂ loses mass in every cycle (sawtooth down), whereas Al₂O₃ gains mass during the WF₆ pulse and barely loses it during the BCl₃ pulse, confirming the formation of a stable, non-volatile fluoride layer on aluminum.

This figure presents QCM data demonstrating material selectivity during WF₆ exposure at 220 °C. The TiO₂ film (red line) shows continuous mass loss, indicating etching, whereas the Al₂O₃ film (black line) exhibits an initial mass gain followed by saturation, indicating no etching.

QCM data comparing WF₆ dosing strategies on TiO₂. Pulsed WF₆ dosing (red line) results in cumulative mass loss (etching), while the initial exposure (black line) is dominated by strong adsorption and fluorination, seen as a large mass gain before etching kinetics dominate.

Equilibrium analysis for the WF₆–TiO₂ system shows a temperature-dependent transition from solid TiF₄(s) to gaseous TiF₄(g) above ~120 °C, enabling etching; at higher temperatures, tungsten oxide byproducts such as WO₃(s) convert to volatile species (e.g., WF₂O₂(g)).
Equilibrium analysis for the WF₆–Al₂O₃ system shows that AlF₃(s) remains solid and stable over the entire temperature range, indicating fluorination without volatilization or etching.

QCM data demonstrating BCl₃ saturation behavior. After an initial WF₆ pulse causes fluorination and mass gain, the first few BCl₃ pulses induce a sharp mass loss. Subsequent BCl₃ pulses produce negligible change, confirming that the etching reaction is self-limiting once the fluorinated surface layer is consumed.

QCM data demonstrating WF₆ saturation behavior. The first WF₆ pulse results in a large mass increase due to surface fluorination, while additional WF₆ pulses contribute minimal mass change, confirming that fluorination is also self-limiting and does not proceed indefinitely.

The figure shows high-resolution XPS Ti 2p binding energy spectra recorded at different ALE cycle numbers, illustrating the progressive reduction and eventual disappearance of the Ti–O doublet as the TiO₂ film is etched away.

The figure shows high-resolution XPS F 1s binding energy spectra measured at different ALE cycle numbers, indicating low and stable fluorine content with only minor metal-fluoride contributions during the etching process.

This figure presents quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) data showing mass changes during chemical exposures at 170 °C. In panel (a), only BCl₃ or WF₆ dosing leads to mass gain or negligible change, and WF₆/Sn(acac)₂ cycling results in net deposition, whereas alternating WF₆ and BCl₃ produces a continuous linear mass loss, confirming atomic layer etching (ALE) of TiO₂.

The image zooms into the ALE process, revealing a sawtooth profile in which the WF₆ pulse increases mass due to surface fluorination, followed by a sharp mass decrease during the BCl₃ pulse from ligand exchange and volatilization, yielding net material removal.

XPS Ti 2p and Si 2p spectra show a linear decrease in the Ti signal and a corresponding increase in the Si substrate signal with increasing WF₆ exposure, confirming progressive thinning and etching of the 5.5 nm TiO₂ film at 220 °C.

XPS W 4f and F 1s spectra increase rapidly and then saturate with WF₆ dosing, indicating the formation of a stable tungsten–fluoride–rich surface layer and associated chemical modification during the process.
This line chart represents the change in film thickness with increasing WF₆ doses at 220 °C, showing a linear decrease that indicates progressive etching or consumption of the 5.5 nm TiO₂ film.

This line chart represents the change in refractive index (at 632.8 nm) with increasing WF₆ doses, showing a sharp initial drop (0–25 doses) followed by stabilization at ~1.85, indicating initial chemical modification before steady-state etching.

TiO₂ System: At 170 °C, the reaction of BCl₃ with fluorinated TiO₂ surface species produces exclusively volatile products such as TiCl₄ (black line) and WOCl₄ (red line), along with solid B₂O₃. The absence of solid titanium species confirms that etching is thermodynamically favorable.

Al₂O₃ System: At 170 °C, aluminum remains as solid AlF₃ (purple line), and significant formation of volatile AlCl₃ (black line) occurs only above 300 °C. This explains the selectivity: Ti-fluorides volatilize at low temperatures, whereas Al-fluorides do not.

In panel (a), only BCl₃ or WF₆ dosing leads to mass gain or negligible change, and WF₆/Sn(acac)₂ cycling results in net deposition, whereas alternating WF₆ and BCl₃ produces a continuous linear mass loss, confirming atomic layer etching (ALE) of TiO₂.

The figure compares thickness and RMS roughness of Al₂O₃ across four processing steps—initial deposition, dry etching, and two pALE cycle conditions. Thickness continuously decreases from ~60 nm to ~10 nm, while roughness first increases after dry etching and then decreases again with pALE, as confirmed by both plotted values and AFM images.

reflects different degrees of peak intensity and binding energy shift, enabling comparison of how plasma environment modifies the chemical state and surface composition of Al₂O₃ during CF₄-based processing.

The figure presents a scatter plot showing how the etching rate of Al₂O₃ during CF₄ dry etching varies with working pressure. The data reveal a steep decline in etching rate as pressure increases from a high rate at near-zero pressure to almost negligible etching above ~1 Torr indicating strong pressure-dependent suppression of etching efficiency.
The XPS survey spectrum displays elemental peaks (Al, O, F, C) across a wide binding-energy range, indicating the chemical composition of the CF₄-etched Al₂O₃ film surface.
The high-resolution Al 2p spectrum shows peak deconvolution into components corresponding to Al–F, Al–O–F, and Al–O bonding states, revealing surface chemical modification after CF₄ plasma exposure.

The figure shows etching per cycle as a function of DMAC pulse time: etching increases rapidly at short pulse times and then approaches a plateau (~0.23–0.25 nm per cycle) beyond about 1 s, indicating saturation of the DMAC dosage.

This panel presents a cross-sectional TEM image of the CF₄ plasma-treated Al₂O₃ film at 0.07 Torr for 10 minutes, accompanied by EDS elemental maps (Al, O, F, Si). The maps reveal partial etching and fluorine incorporation near the surface, while the dotted line marks the original Al₂O₃ thickness.

This panel shows the same set of analyses for a higher-pressure CF₄ treatment (2 Torr for 5 minutes). The TEM image displays a significantly reduced etching depth, and the EDS maps indicate a more uniform elemental distribution with less fluorine penetration compared to the low-pressure condition.

This panel shows a tilted SEM image of the pristine porous template at 0 ALD cycles, revealing a highly ordered, interconnected pore network with sharp edges and uniform feature size, serving as the reference morphology before film deposition.

This top-view SEM image at higher magnification illustrates the open pore structure of the uncoated template, with well-defined pore openings and thin pore walls, indicating minimal surface roughness prior to ALD processing.

This high-magnification SEM image highlights the local pore geometry of the uncoated structure, showing irregular pore interiors and sharp junctions that emphasize the complexity of the high-aspect-ratio surface to be coated.

This tilted SEM image after 100 ALD cycles shows that the overall porous architecture is preserved while pore walls become visibly thicker, demonstrating conformal film growth without structural collapse.
This top-view SEM image after 100 ALD cycles reveals a reduction in pore diameter and smoother pore edges, indicating uniform coating of the internal pore surfaces by the ALD film.
This high-magnification image further confirms conformal deposition after 100 cycles, with increased wall thickness and rounded pore contours resulting from continuous ALD film growth.

This tilted SEM image after 300 ALD cycles shows substantial thickening of the pore walls while maintaining the long-range order of the porous structure, highlighting the scalability of conformal growth with increasing cycle number.
This top-view SEM image after 300 cycles demonstrates significant pore narrowing and enhanced uniformity, reflecting cumulative ALD film deposition on all exposed surfaces.

This high-magnification SEM image illustrates near-cylindrical pores with smooth, thick walls after 300 cycles, confirming excellent thickness control and conformality even after extensive ALD processing.

The figure compares Al₂O₃ film thickness across increasing pALE cycle numbers at four different temperatures. Higher temperatures (350–400°C) show significantly greater film removal per cycle, while lower temperatures (250–300°C) show slower and less pronounced thinning, indicating a clear temperature-dependent etch rate.

The figure presents a saturation curve showing how the etching-per-cycle rate increases sharply during the initial seconds of CF₄ plasma treatment and then gradually plateaus as the exposure time reaches 20 seconds. This indicates a self-limiting etching behavior characteristic of atomic layer processes.

The figure schematically illustrates the ALE window as a function of ion energy, positioned between spontaneous chemical etching at low ion energies and physical sputter etching at high ion energies. Within this window, chemically enhanced, directional etching occurs when the ion energy is above the chemical etching threshold (E₍etch₎) but below the physical sputtering threshold (E₍psh₎), enabling controlled atomic-scale material removal.

The C 1s XPS spectrum of the thin fluorocarbon film after deposition shows dominant C–C/C–H and C–CF bonding contributions, with relatively weak C–F₂ and C–F₃ components, indicating a low fluorine content and a polymer structure rich in hydrocarbon-like bonds.

The C 1s XPS spectrum of the thick fluorocarbon film after deposition exhibits stronger C–F, C–F₂, and C–F₃ peak intensities compared to the thin film, demonstrating increased fluorination and a higher degree of CFₓ bonding as film thickness increases.

The figure presents real-time ellipsometry trajectories (Δ vs Ψ) comparing cyclic ALE with continuous Ar/C₄F₈ plasma etching during processing of a SiO₂–Si–SiO₂ multilayer stack. The cyclic ALE trajectory exhibits discrete, stepwise movement corresponding to controlled layer-by-layer processing, whereas continuous etching shows a smoother, continuous trajectory, indicating different surface reaction pathways and selectivity between top SiO₂, Si, and bottom SiO₂ layers.

This panel shows the SiO₂ thickness change during a cyclic plasma process at 25 eV ion energy, where initial fluorocarbon deposition causes a positive thickness change followed by net etching during ion exposure, demonstrating time-resolved alternating deposition and removal behavior.

At a higher ion energy of 30 eV, this panel reveals enhanced SiO₂ etching compared to 25 eV, with a deeper negative thickness change during the etching phase, indicating stronger ion-assisted material removal.

This panel compares fluorocarbon and SiFx deposition with underlying Si thickness evolution at 25 eV ion energy, showing that fluorocarbon accumulation dominates while the Si substrate experiences minimal thickness loss, indicating selective surface modification.

At 30 eV ion energy, this panel shows substantial Si thickness reduction alongside fluorocarbon and SiFx evolution, demonstrating that higher ion energy increases substrate etching and reduces selectivity.
shows cycle-to-cycle evolution of plasma potential during C₄F₈ pulsing, where longer pulse duration (5.0 s) induces larger and more persistent potential changes than the shorter pulse (1.5 s).
presents the electron density response, indicating higher and more slowly relaxing density for longer C₄F₈ exposure due to increased residual fluorocarbon species.
shows changes in electron temperature, which remain relatively low but exhibit stronger perturbations for longer pulse duration, reflecting altered plasma conditions.
This panel shows the initial hydroxyl-terminated substrate surface, where surface –OH groups act as reactive sites for precursor adsorption at the beginning of the ALD cycle.
In this panel, precursor molecules containing –CH₃ ligands are introduced and react with surface –OH groups until saturation is reached, forming a chemisorbed layer and releasing reaction byproducts.
This panel illustrates the purge step after precursor exposure, during which excess precursor molecules and gaseous byproducts are removed, leaving a stable adsorbed layer on the surface.
Here, a uniformly saturated adsorbed precursor layer is shown on the substrate, indicating completion of the self-limiting adsorption step.

This panel depicts exposure of the adsorbed layer to a reactive plasma gas, where energetic species react with the surface-bound ligands until saturation, converting the adsorbed layer into the desired solid material.
In this panel, a second purge step removes residual reactive gas species and reaction byproducts formed during plasma exposure, preventing undesired gas-phase reactions.
The final panel shows the regenerated surface with new –OH termination, ready for the next ALD cycle, demonstrating controlled layer-by-layer film growth.

The figure schematically illustrates the thermal ALD window, showing how thickness growth per cycle depends on process temperature. In the central ALD window, surface reactions are self-limiting and yield constant growth per cycle. At low temperatures, condensation and incomplete reactions dominate, while at high temperatures, desorption and precursor decomposition lead to loss of ALD control.

This panel shows the initial clean substrate surface with available reactive sites prior to any precursor exposure, representing the starting state of the plasma-enhanced atomic layer etching or deposition cycle.
In this panel, precursor molecules are introduced and adsorb onto the substrate surface, forming a chemisorbed layer through self-limiting surface reactions.
This panel illustrates the purge step following precursor exposure, during which excess and weakly bound precursor molecules are removed from the chamber, leaving only the adsorbed surface layer.
Here, a saturated adsorbed layer uniformly covers the substrate surface, indicating completion of the precursor adsorption step and readiness for activation.
This panel depicts low-energy ion bombardment during plasma exposure, which selectively activates surface reactions by removing or modifying the adsorbed layer through ion-assisted processes.
In this panel, a second purge step removes reaction byproducts and desorbed species generated during ion activation, preventing gas-phase reactions.
The final panel shows the substrate after completion of one full cycle, with the surface reset for the next cycle and a controlled, layer-by-layer material modification achieved.

The figure schematically illustrates the dependence of etched thickness per cycle on reactant exposure time under different reactant pressures. Curves (i), (ii), and (iii) represent increasing pressure conditions, showing that higher pressure leads to faster attainment of saturation surface coverage, which is required for self-limiting atomic layer etching.

The figure shows the in situ thickness evolution of an SiO₂ film during multiple atomic layer etching (ALE) cycles. Each cycle consists of pulsed C₄F₈ exposure followed by Ar ion bombardment, resulting in a stepwise and repeatable decrease in thickness that demonstrates controlled, cycle-by-cycle material removal.

This figure compiles experimental literature data and SRIM simulation results showing the physical sputter yield of SiO₂ as a function of the square root of Ar⁺ ion energy. The data reveal a clear sputtering threshold around ~45 eV, above which the sputter yield increases approximately linearly with ion energy. The wide scatter among experimental points highlights strong sensitivity to experimental conditions such as surface state, angle of incidence, and ion flux, emphasizing challenges in precise sputter control at low energies.

This figure presents a comparative scatter plot of experimentally reported physical sputter yields of silicon (Si) as a function of the square root of Ar⁺ ion energy, compiled from multiple literature sources. The wide dispersion of data points highlights strong experiment-to-experiment variability, particularly near the sputtering threshold. A SRIM simulation curve is included as a reference, indicating an approximate threshold energy of ~20 eV for Si sputtering. The figure emphasizes the inherent difficulty in achieving precise control in physical sputtering processes.

The line chart shows the cumulative mass change over 30 ALE cycles, displaying a linear downward trend (negative slope), which indicates a constant etch rate throughout the process.

Mass Change Per Cycle (MCPC) (ng/(cm²·cycle)) varies the TMA pulse time (with fixed 4s NF₃). Both plots show the mass loss saturating, proving each half-reaction is self-limiting.

The left panel shows the oxidation half-cycle: O₃ reacts with TiN, oxidizing Ti³⁺ to Ti⁴⁺ and forming a self-limiting TiO₂ surface layer. Gaseous O₂, NOₓ, or N₂ are released as byproducts.

The right panel shows the fluorination half-cycle: HF reacts with the TiO₂ layer, producing volatile TiF₄ and H₂O that desorb from the surface. The dashed blue line indicates the net etched layer thickness per cycle.
The chart shows the mass change over time for 10 HF exposures on ALD TiO₂ at different temperatures (200°C, 250°C, and 300°C). The mass decreases over time, indicating a loss of material

The figure demonstrates selective TiN etching using O₃/HF at 250°C. TiN (gray diamonds) shows linear thickness reduction while Al₂O₃, HfO₂, ZrO₂, SiO₂, and Si₃N₄ remain essentially unchanged over 400 cycles. This confirms high selectivity of the ALE process for TiN over common dielectrics and barrier materials.

This figure shows XRR scans for TiN films before and after various numbers of ALE cycles using O₃ and HF at 250°C. Six scans are displayed: initial TiN (a), and after 50 (b), 100 (c), 150 (d), 200 (e), and 300 (f) ALE cycles. The interference fringes progressively change with cycle number, reflecting linear film thickness reduction at approximately 0.20 Å/cycle. The curves also reveal decreasing surface roughness from 9.3 Å initially to 7.5 Å after 100 cycles.

This figure displays XRR scans for TiN films before and after different numbers of thermal ALE cycles using H₂O₂ and HF at 250°C. Five scans are shown: initial TiN (a), and after 50 (b), 100 (c), 200 (d), and 400 (e) ALE cycles. The progressive changes in the curves demonstrate systematic film thickness reduction with increasing cycles, confirming effective etching at 0.15 Å/cycle. The interference fringe evolution also indicates surface smoothing during the ALE process.
The line chart shows the film thickness in Ångströms (Å) over the number of cycles for XRR and SE methods at 250°C using H₂O₂ and HF
The figure shows FTIR absorbance spectra comparing MoF₆ and H₂O exposure effects. Panel (a) shows minimal absorbance for MoF₆, with prominent absorbance bands for H₂O near 1000–800 cm⁻¹.
Panel (b) reveals characteristic Mo-F and Mo-O vibrational bands, suggesting the formation of metal-oxygen and metal-fluorine bonds post-exposure

The bar chart compares the etch rates of materials in crystalline and amorphous phases. The etch rate for the amorphous phase is significantly higher (0.5 Å/cycle) than that of the crystalline phase (0.2 Å/cycle), indicating that amorphous materials are more susceptible to etching under the same process conditions
Panel (a) shows the mass loss (ng/cm²) of material as a function of MoF₆ dose duration, revealing rapid initial etching followed by saturation beyond ~1 second.

Panel (b) plots mass loss against H₂O dose time, similarly showing an initial sharp increase that levels off near 4 seconds. The graphs indicate self-limiting behaviour characteristic of atomic layer etching (ALE), where further dosing does not result in additional material removal beyond a certain threshold.

Panels (a)  shows S 2p XPS spectra at different exposure times, with fitted components and envelope curves revealing shifts in chemical state. The figure also displays Mo 3d spectra under different conditions, showing multiple Mo oxidation states and good fit quality, evidenced by the low residuals above each spectrum

The line chart depicts mass change during sequential deposition and etching stages over elapsed time. The deposition phase (blue arrow) shows a steady increase in mass, while the etching phase (orange arrow) leads to a gradual decrease, indicating material removal.

The line chart illustrates real-time mass changes during alternating exposures to H₂O and MoF₆, visualised through stepwise increases and decreases. Annotated bars indicate the timing of each precursor pulse, and labeled Δ values quantify the mass gain and loss per cycle.

The line chart displays the effect of temperature on the mass change per cycle during a process governed by the recipe "1/20/2.5/20". As the temperature increases from 100°C to 300°C, the mass change becomes increasingly negative, indicating greater mass loss per cycle at higher temperatures. This trend suggests that etching becomes more efficient or aggressive as the temperature rises, especially beyond 200°C.
Panel (a) shows no change in thickness when only Hhfac is used, even after 20 pulses.

The figure shows Raman spectra of MoS₂ before and after etching corresponds to the control, 75 cycles etched, and 90 cycles etched samples, respectively. Each spectrum displays two prominent peaks corresponding to the in-plane (E²g) and out-of-plane (A₁g) vibrational modes of MoS₂. With increasing etching cycles, the separation between these peaks decreases from 25.5 cm⁻¹ to 22 cm⁻¹, suggesting a reduction in the number of MoS₂ layers or structural modification due to etching.

Depth-profiling XPS data for the original TiN film showing atomic concentrations versus Ar sputtering time and estimated depth. At the surface, oxygen (~16%) and carbon (~12%) are elevated due to native oxide and adventitious carbon. After ~60 s of sputtering (~2 nm depth), concentrations plateau to bulk values: Ti (~49%), N (~42%), O (~6%), C (~2%), and F (~1%).

Depth-profiling XPS data for the ALE-treated TiN film. The surface shows significantly lower oxygen (~8%) compared to the original film, while fluorine is slightly elevated (~6.5%). Bulk composition remains essentially unchanged from the original film, confirming that ALE affects only the top few nanometers.

Schematic illustrating the two-step plasma-thermal ALE cycle for TiN. First, molecular oxygen (blue dots) oxidizes the TiN surface to form a self-limiting TiO2 layer. Second, SF6/H2 plasma generates in-situ HF (green dots) that selectively etches the TiO2 while leaving the underlying TiN intact. The dashed outline in the final panel indicates the removed material, showing net thickness reduction after one complete cycle.

the multiple stacked line chart represents the time-resolved Quadrupole Mass Spectrometry (QMS) data collected over 5 full cycles of ZnS Atomic Layer Etching (ALE) at 275°C using HF and TMA , show clear, repeatable signals for each half-reaction. The first TMA pulse generates the 68Zn–(CH₃)₂⁺ ion at m/z 98, while HF exposure produces the H₂S⁺ signal at m/z 34. Later TMA pulses also generate the Al₂(CH₃)₄F⁺ ion at m/z 133, confirming the expected reaction by-products.

The line chart shows the relationship between the number of ALE cycles and the RMS roughness of a material. As the number of ALE cycles increases, the RMS roughness initially decreases slightly and then sharply increases.

The muliple line chart  shows time-resolved QMS ion intensities during five sequential HF pulses to fresh ZnS powder at 275 °C. The HF⁺ signal (m/z 20) displays clear, square-wave pulses, confirming consistent and controlled delivery of HF during each exposure. The H₂S⁺ signal (m/z 34), expected from a ZnS–HF exchange reaction, remains at the baseline with no detectable peaks. This indicates that HF alone does not react with or etch fresh ZnS under these conditions, implying that surface activation or a second precursor is required for ZnS ALE.

Multiple line chart shows QMS spectra recorded during the fourth cycle of ZnS ALE at 275 °C for HF and TMA exposures, displayed in three different mass-to-charge (m/z) regions. The spectrum in the m/z range of 10–40 compares ion intensities during HF and TMA exposures, highlighting low-mass fragments associated with the reactant and volatile byproducts.

The spectrum in the m/z range of 60–100 focuses on intermediate-mass ions, where species such as Al(CH₃)F⁺ and Zn(CH₃)⁺ are detected, indicating the formation of volatile zinc- and aluminum-containing reaction products during the TMA step.

The spectrum in the m/z range of 100–215 highlights higher-mass fragments, including aluminum fluoride–methyl complexes such as Al₂F(CH₃)₄⁺ and Al₃F₃(CH₃)₅⁺, confirming fluorine removal through the formation of volatile aluminum–fluoride species.

The multi-line chart shows how the etch rate (Å/cycle) of ZnS ALD changes with HF pressures for depositions carried out at TMA pressures of 20 m.Torr and 200 m.Torr. As the pressure increases till  50, the etch rate increases and plateus.

The multiple-line chart shows the thickness change of zinc sulfide thin films as a function of the number of ZnS ALE cycles measured at 300 °C. The etch rate is approximately 2 Å per cycle. The data correspond to initial ZnS film thicknesses of 20, 40, and 60 nm, where the original ZnS ALD films were deposited at 100 °C.

The multiple line chart shows the reduction in ZnS film thickness with increasing ALE cycles (0–20) at temperatures from 225 °C to 300 °C for films initially deposited at 100 °C. All values display a linear decrease in thickness, confirming a constant etch per cycle and a well-controlled, self-limiting ALE process. The slope increases with temperature, indicating thermally activated etching, with the highest etch rate at 300 °C (~2.1 Å/cycle) and the lowest at 225 °C (~1.25 Å/cycle).

This muliple line chart  shows the etch rate per cycle (Å/cycle) as a function of precursor exposure time for two different ALE process conditions: one varying HF exposure time (x) with fixed TMA time (4 s), and the other varying TMA exposure time (y) with fixed HF time (1 s). Both show a rapid increase in etch rate with exposure time up to ~2 seconds, followed by a saturation plateau, indicating self-limiting surface reactions.

The multi-line chart shows how the etch rate (Å/cycle) of ZnS ALD changes with temperature for depositions carried out at 100 °C, 200 °C, and 300 °C. As the temperature increases, the etch rate also increases.The film deposited at 100 °C etches the fastest, while the film deposited at 300 °C etches the slowest at any given temperature. This implies that films deposited at higher temperatures are denser or chemically more robust.

The multi axis chart shows time-resolved QMS ion intensities during the TMA pulse. Volatile zinc byproducts, ⁶⁸Zn(CH₃)⁺ (m/z 83) and ⁶⁸Zn(CH₃)₂⁺ (m/z 98), appear immediately at the start of the pulse and decay rapidly, well before the TMA signal (m/z 72) saturates. This indicates a fast, self-limiting surface reaction that releases dimethylzinc.

The multi axis bottom chart shows the aluminum-fluoride dimer fragment Al₂(CH₃)₄F⁺ (m/z 133) relative to the TMA signal (m/z 72). The product signal spikes instantly at the beginning of the TMA exposure, confirming rapid removal of surface fluorine through volatile dimethylaluminum fluoride formation, occurring concurrently with zinc removal.

This diagram shows one complete atomic layer etching (ALEt) cycle. The surface first reacts with a precursor in a self-limiting adsorption step. After a purge, a co-reactant activates the modified surface in another self-limiting step, followed by a final purge. When both exposures reach saturation, exactly one atomic layer is removed per cycle.

This diagram shows the same sequence applied to atomic layer deposition (ALD). The key difference is that the activation step leads to material addition instead of removal. When both half-reactions are saturated, each cycle adds a fixed amount of material to the surface.

These saturation curves explain why exposure times matter. If exposure is too short, the surface is under-saturated and the process is incomplete. If exposure is too long between steps, unwanted parasitic CVD or etching can occur. Proper saturation ensures true atomic-layer behavior.

The schematic illustrates how ALEt enables atomic-layer-controlled removal while emphasizing selectivity and directional behavior. Anisotropic ALEt removes material preferentially from horizontal surfaces, whereas isotropic ALEt removes material uniformly from all exposed surfaces. Uniformity and etch control are highlighted as key outcomes of the layer-by-layer, surface-limited process.

The ALD schematic shows atomic-layer addition with excellent uniformity on planar substrates and high conformality inside three-dimensional structures. Because ALD relies on self-limiting surface chemistry, growth per cycle remains consistent across the feature geometry, enabling precise thickness control and uniform coverage of high-aspect-ratio structures.

The continuous process diagram shows thickness increasing with time during constant exposure to process A. Because the process is flux-controlled, small changes in gas flow or conditions can strongly affect the final thickness.

The pulsed process diagram shows that exposure to process A is divided into repeated pulses. This gives better timing control than a continuous process, but the thickness per pulse still depends on how much material arrives at the surface.

The atomic layer process diagram shows alternating A and B steps that react only with the surface. Once each step is saturated, every cycle adds the same fixed amount of material, allowing very accurate thickness control by counting cycles.

The panel illustrates an inherent selectivity mechanism for area-selective ALD in which certain substrate regions support rapid nucleation while others show negligible nucleation, resulting in selective film growth without masking.

The analogous inherent selectivity concept for ALEt is shown, where the chosen etch chemistry removes one material significantly faster than adjacent materials, producing selective etching without additional activation or masking.

This panel shows selective deposition achieved by surface deactivation. A deactivating layer or molecular film blocks precursor adsorption, preventing ALD growth on protected areas while allowing nucleation on unmasked regions.

Selective ALEt by deactivation is illustrated: a masking layer suppresses etching on the protected surfaces, enabling selective removal only from the exposed material. Etch selectivity depends on the much higher etch rate of the target material relative to the mask.

The panel depicts selective ALD by activation, where only surfaces locally activated—e.g., by focused electron or ion beams—initiate nucleation. Activation is required only before the first ALD cycle for many processes.

Selective ALEt by activation is shown, in which activation must occur each cycle to enable removal on targeted regions. Unactivated surfaces remain chemically inert and do not undergo material removal.
The schematic shows a regular ALD/ALEt cycle (AB)m in which self-limiting A and B half-reactions are alternated for m cycles. Each cycle delivers a fixed growth or etch per cycle once saturated.

The multistep cycle (ABC)m introduces additional half-reactions, enabling tailoring of material composition or surface chemistry by adding a third reactant step while retaining cyclic self-limiting behavior.

The supercycle schematic illustrates periodic repetition of two different regular processes. By tuning m and n for the (A1B1)m(A2B2)n combination, the final material properties or average growth/etch rate can be engineered with nanometer-scale precision.

The bottom-left diagram explains throughput enhancement via reduced exposure times. Operating slightly below saturation shortens cycle duration while sacrificing only a small fraction of growth or etch per cycle, offering acceptable control in processes tolerant to minor deviations from saturation.

The spatial ALD/ALE concept depicts precursor, co-reactant, and purge zones arranged along a substrate translation path. Instead of time-sequenced half-reactions, the substrate encounters spatially separated reaction environments, enabling high-throughput continuous processing.

This dual-panel figure illustrates how MCPC (mass change per cycle) varies with precursor exposure time for both SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂ in the Al₂O₃ ALE process. In both cases, the MCPC decreases rapidly with increasing exposure time and asymptotically approaches a saturation point, indicating that each half-reaction becomes self-limiting beyond a certain exposure threshold. These saturation behaviors confirm that both precursors operate via self-limiting surface chemistry, a defining feature of atomic layer etching processes.

The figure presents X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data for a vanadium oxide sample, with signal intensity plotted against binding energy. Peaks corresponding to the O 1s and vanadium V 2p orbitals (V 2p₁/₂ and V 2p₃/₂) are deconvoluted into their constituent oxidation states. The red and blue curves indicate the contributions from V(IV) and V(V) states, respectively. Quantitative analysis reveals that 94.4% of vanadium is in the +4 oxidation state, while only 5.6% exists as V(V). This suggests a dominant VO₂-like phase in the sample, with minimal overoxidation to V₂O₅.

The figure presents a mass change profile over time during a VO₂ atomic layer etching (ALE) process conducted at 200 °C. The mass oscillations correspond to alternating exposures of SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂, clearly indicated by arrows. Each cycle begins with a mass loss during SF₄ exposure, suggesting etching, followed by a mass gain during Sn(acac)₂ dosing, indicative of precursor adsorption. The overall trend highlights the stepwise nature of the ALE process, where the mass change remains tightly controlled and periodic, showcasing the self-limiting behavior typical of atomic layer processing.

This line graph displays the mass change of a VO₂ film during atomic layer deposition (ALD) at 150 °C over the course of 125 cycles. The increase in mass is linear with respect to time, indicating a highly consistent and reproducible deposition process. The graph annotates a growth rate of 0.63 Å/cycle, and the pulse sequence for the precursors (TEMAV and H₂O) is noted as part of the experimental procedure. The steady mass gain confirms the self-limiting, layer-by-layer nature of ALD and supports the reliability of the chosen conditions for high-quality VO₂ film growth.

The figure presents the film thickness of Al₂O₃ as a function of the number of atomic layer etching (ALE) cycles at four different temperatures: 150 °C, 175 °C, 200 °C, and 225 °C. Each dataset shows a monotonic decrease in thickness with increasing cycles, but the rate of etching significantly depends on temperature. At 150 °C, the change is minimal, indicating very slow etching, while the steepest decrease is observed at 200 °C and 225 °C, demonstrating a higher etch rate. The linearity in most cases suggests a consistent per-cycle removal, particularly at elevated temperatures.

The line chart illustrates mass change (in ng/cm²) as a function of time during the atomic layer etching (ALE) of VO₂ at 200°C. The etching process involves alternating exposures to SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂, as labeled by the arrows on the plot. Distinct mass loss and gain events are observed during the SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂ steps, respectively. The graph captures both the etching half-cycle (mass loss from SF₄) and the subsequent precursor adsorption step (mass gain from Sn(acac)₂), culminating in a repeatable etch pattern. The label "MCPC" denotes the minimum mass point per cycle.

This line chart presents mass change data during Al₂O₃ atomic layer etching (ALE) at 200°C, showing stepwise decreases in mass following alternating exposures to SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂. Distinct mass loss events (ΔM) occur upon each SF₄ pulse, indicating volatile byproduct formation from fluorination. The subsequent Sn(acac)₂ exposures yield mass gains, consistent with precursor adsorption. The black dashed arrow labeled MCPC highlights a stable mass plateau following precursor saturation, marking the point of maximum chemisorbed precursor coverage.

The figure shows mass gain over time during fluorination of VO₂ at 200 °C using three different conditions: SF₄ at ~500 mTorr, HF at ~500 mTorr, and HF at ~60 mTorr. All three conditions exhibit a sharp increase in mass at around 30 s, indicating fluorine uptake, followed by a plateau, suggesting saturation. SF₄ leads to the highest mass increase (~32 ng/cm²), followed by HF at higher pressure (~18 ng/cm²) and HF at lower pressure (~10 ng/cm²). These differences highlight the influence of both fluorine source and pressure on the extent of fluorination.

This figure compares the infrared absorbance spectra of VO₂ films fluorinated using SF₄ and HF gases at 200 °C. Each trace represents the progression of exposure from 1 to 10 cycles, showing how the spectral features evolve with increasing fluorination. The SF₄ spectra (in blue) exhibit a progressive increase in the V–F absorbance region (500–800 cm⁻¹) and attenuation in the V=O region (~1000 cm⁻¹), indicating successful replacement of oxygen with fluorine. The HF spectra (in red) also show similar trends but with reduced intensity changes, suggesting lower fluorine incorporation compared to SF₄.

The chart presents the mass change over time during atomic layer etching (ALE) of Al₂O₃ at 200 °C. The process alternates between SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂ exposures, with each reagent inducing distinct mass gain or loss steps. The mass increases sharply following each Sn(acac)₂ dose and decreases following SF₄ exposure, consistent with a ligand-exchange-driven etch mechanism. The repeatability of these mass shifts across cycles demonstrates the controlled and cyclical nature of the ALE process.

The figure presents FTIR absorbance spectra collected during fluorination of Al₂O₃ using SF₄ (blue) and HF (red) at 200 °C over 10 exposure cycles. Each spectrum shows how absorbance changes with increasing exposure, highlighting characteristic peaks for Al–O and Al–F bonding. For SF₄, the Al–F signal grows consistently with cycle number, while for HF, Al–O peaks diminish and Al–F peaks grow, indicating progressive surface fluorination. The separation of the two data sets allows direct comparison of chemical evolution during ALE processes.

The line chart shows the mass change during atomic layer etching (ALE) of VO₂ at 200 °C over 50 cycles. A steady linear decrease in mass is observed, reaching approximately –600 ng/cm² by 5000 seconds. The etch rate is specified as 0.30 Å/cycle, and the process involves alternating pulses of SF₄ and Sn(acac)₂, each with pulse and purge times indicated as "3–55–3–55". The linear profile confirms self-limiting and uniform removal per cycle, consistent with ideal ALE behaviour.

This figure shows a series of FTIR absorbance spectra collected during the atomic layer deposition (ALD) of VO₂ at 150 °C. The spectra are plotted as absorbance vs. wavenumber (cm⁻¹) and correspond to increasing ALD cycle numbers, ranging from 5 to 35. Two primary peaks are observed: one near 1010 cm⁻¹ corresponding to the V=O stretching mode, and another near 700–600 cm⁻¹ associated with the V–O bond in VO₂. The increasing intensity of both peaks with cycle number indicates progressive film growth and the formation of VO₂ bonds over successive ALD cycles.

The histogram shows that atomic chlorine (>40%) is the dominant ejected species, indicating significant sputtering of adsorbed chlorine without effective etching. Actual etching occurs via SiCl and SiCl₂ fragments (~35%), while the presence of pure Si (~16%) reveals unintended physical sputtering of the underlying silicon by 70 eV ions.

The histogram shows that early in the Ar⁺ bombardment, etching is chemically driven, dominated by highly chlorinated species (mainly SiCl₂) from a saturated surface layer, with minimal pure Si. In the later phase, the chlorinated layer is depleted and the process shifts to physical sputtering, evidenced by a sharp increase in pure Si and leaner SiCl products, while a large fraction of atomic Cl indicates significant inefficiency due to sputtered etchant. Early phase: Etching is chemically driven, dominated by highly chlorinated species (mainly SiCl₂) from a chlorine-saturated surface layer, with negligible pure Si.

Late phase: The chlorinated layer is depleted, and etching shifts to physical sputtering, marked by increased pure Si and leaner SiCl products, with significant atomic Cl indicating sputtered etchant inefficiency.

The figure shows silicon ALE using cyclic Cl₂ adsorption and Ar⁺ bombardment, producing a characteristic stair-step etch profile. Material removal occurs only during the ion step, confirming the self-limiting ALE sequence. The etch per cycle increases with ion energy, from ~3 Å at 40 eV to ~11 Å at 100 eV after 8 cycles, indicating that higher energies may exceed the monolayer-limited regime.

The scatter plot compares simulated and experimental etch per cycle (EPC) as a function of Ar⁺ ion irradiation time at 70 eV. Both datasets show EPC increasing with time before saturating, with the simulation (red circles) closely matching the trend and saturation value (~1.36 Å/cycle) of the experimental results (blue squares).

This graph illustrates the cyclic nature of the Atomic Layer Etching (ALE) process over 8 cycles. The "sawtooth" pattern represents the alternating addition of Chlorine (rising slope) and removal of material via Argon ion bombardment (falling slope). The distinct colored lines show that higher ion energies (e.g., 100 eV) result in deeper removal of the chlorinated layer compared to lower energies (e.g., 40 eV).

This figure displays the depth profiles (density vs. Z-position) of Silicon (blue) and Chlorine (red) during an ALE cycle. Panel (a) shows the state immediately after chlorination but before ion bombardment, where a significant chlorine-rich layer exists at the surface (high Z).
The label shows the state after 150 Argon ion impacts, revealing how the ion bombardment has begun to remove the surface material and push some chlorine deeper into the silicon lattice.

The scatter plot shows the relationship between the number of Cl₂ impacts and etch per cycle.  The EPC initially increases rapidly with chlorine dose due to incomplete surface coverage, then saturates beyond ~1000 Cl₂ i impacts at ~0.9 Å/cycle. This plateau demonstrates the self-limiting behavior of ideal atomic layer etching, where additional chlorine does not increase material removal once the surface is fully chlorinated.

The multiple line graph shows the density of Si and Cl atoms at different Z positions along the y-axis, indicating that after the first chlorination step Cl remains near the surface while Si dominates the bulk.

The multiple line graph shows the density of Si and Cl atoms at different Z positions along the y-axis, indicating that after the first ion bombardment step the near-surface Si density decreases and the Cl profile becomes slightly redistributed.

The multiple line graph shows the density of Si and Cl atoms at different Z positions along the y-axis, indicating that after the fourth chlorination step Cl penetration into the near-surface region increases while Si density near the surface is further reduced.

The multiple line graph shows the density of Si and Cl atoms at different Z positions along the y-axis, indicating that after the fourth ion bombardment step Si is further sputtered from the surface and the Cl distribution becomes more diffuse.

The multiple line graph shows the density of Si and Cl atoms at different Z positions along the y-axis, indicating that after the eighth chlorination step a pronounced Cl-rich surface layer forms with a corresponding depletion of Si near the interface.

The multiple line graph shows the density of Si and Cl atoms at different Z positions along the y-axis, indicating that after the eighth ion bombardment step the surface Si density continues to decrease while the Cl profile is partially flattened due to repeated sputtering and mixing.

The scatter plot represents that Atomic Layer Etching produces a much higher EPC than physical sputtering at the same ion energies, demonstrating the advantage of chlorine surface modification. An optimal “ALE window” (~70–90 eV) allows selective removal of the modified layer without damaging bulk silicon, while sufficient ion fluence is essential, as low fluence yields negligible etching despite chlorination. Etch per cycle is shown as a function of Ar⁺ ion energy. For the “High Fluence” simulation results, one cycle corresponds to 2255 Cl₂ impacts and 1000 Ar⁺ impacts, while for “Low Fluence,” one cycle corresponds to 2255 Cl₂ impacts and 68 Ar⁺ impacts. Solid red circles represent physical sputtering results from MD simulations, open red circles represent ALE results from the “High Fluence” MD simulations, and open red triangles represent ALE results from the “Low Fluence” MD simulations. Solid blue squares are experimental physical sputtering results, while open blue squares are experimental ALE results, both from Park et al. (Ref. 11). The dotted lines indicate the experimentally identified ALE window.

The figure illustrates a surface reaction mechanism, likely a half-cycle of Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) or Etching. It depicts precursor molecules approaching the surface (indicated by downward dashed arrows), reacting with the green and red lattice sites, and the subsequent release of volatile byproducts into the gas phase (indicated by upward dashed arrows).

This figure plots the thermodynamic contributions to the reaction free energy profile (FEP) for the etching reaction TiO₂(bulk) + 4HF(g) → TiF₄(g) + 2H₂O(g) as a function of temperature (0–800 K) at a constant HF pressure of 0.2 Torr. It shows how the electronic energy (ΔE), zero-point correction (ΔE+ΔZPE), Gibbs free energy (ΔG), and entropic term (TΔS) evolve with temperature.

This multiple line chart image compares the free energy profiles (FEPs) of three Continuous Vapor Etching (CVE) reactions (red, red-dashed, blue) with their corresponding self-limiting (SL) surface modification reactions (green, green-dashed, yellow). For ideal Atomic Layer Etching (ALE), the SL reaction must be spontaneous (ΔG < 0) while the CVE reaction remains non-spontaneous (ΔG > 0), defining a “Goldilocks” window where the surface is modified but bulk etching is suppressed.

This figure displays the infrared difference spectra of a Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂) film reacting with Hydrofluoric Acid (HF) at 280 °C. The data tracks the evolution of surface chemistry over 10 sequential exposures. The key finding is the progressive growth of a negative absorbance feature (valley) at 777 cm⁻¹, which identifies as the Ti–F stretching vibration, confirming that HF is successfully fluorinating the oxide surface.

This figure shows time-resolved mass spectrometry data for various titanium fluoride ion fragments (Ti⁺, TiF⁺, TiF₂⁺, TiF₃⁺, TiF₄⁺) during a 2-minute HF exposure on a sample at 300°C. All TiFₓ⁺ signals rise and fall synchronously with the HF pulse, confirming their origin as volatile products of the etching reaction.

The figure compares the surface response from the first HF exposure (blue difference spectrum) with the bulk TiO₂ absorbance (red ALD envelope). A Ti–F stretching feature at ~750–777 cm⁻¹ in the blue curve confirms surface fluorination, while its overlap with the Ti–O/Ti–O–Ti modes in the red curve indicates similar vibrational frequencies for surface and bulk bonds.

The spectral chart represents the Infra red absorbance values ofTiO₂ at 200°C. The graph is represented with various ALE cycles . These FTIR spectra were referenced to the initial TiO ,. The wavelength range used here is 400–1400 cm⁻¹

This figure presents quadrupole mass spectrometry (QMS) data during HF exposure of titanium dioxide (TiO₂) at 300 °C.  The label shows dominant TiF₃⁺, TiF₂⁺, and TiF⁺ peaks, indicating the formation and desorption of volatile titanium fluoride etch products
This label  (b) confirms the TiF₃⁺ signal at m/z = 105 by its excellent match between measured data and the predicted titanium isotopic pattern.

This figure shows real-time mass spectrometry (MS) data during a 2-minute HF exposure at 300 °C.  This image shows a simultaneous H₂O output signal that appears only during HF exposure, confirming water formation as a direct reaction byproduct (TiO₂ + 4HF → TiF₄ + 2H₂O).
The spectral image displays the HF input signal, which rises at ~6.2 min, remains constant, and drops at ~8.1 min.

This figure plots the Free Energy Profiles (FEPs) for the CVE1 (continuous vapor etch) and SL1 (self-limiting surface reaction) pathways for HF interacting with TiO₂. The lines cross at 360 K, defining three regimes: below 360 K, SL1 is favored (self-limiting); between 360 K and ~700 K, CVE1 becomes progressively more favored (preferred etching); above 700 K, CVE1 is strongly favored (purely etching).

This figure uses heat maps to visualize the Gibbs free energy (ΔG) for continuous etching (CVE1) and self-limiting modification (SL1) as a function of temperature (X-axis) and reactant pressure (Y-axis). A contour plot showing the reaction conditions favorable or unfavorable for continuous etching (CVE1) across varying temperatures and reactant pressures. Panel (a) shows that CVE1 is “favorable” (green, ΔG < 0) across nearly the entire parameter space, indicating TiO₂ is naturally unstable in HF.
A contour plot showing the reaction conditions favorable or unfavorable for self-limiting modification (SL1) across varying temperatures and reactant pressures.

A contour plot showing the difference between the two reactions (Y1 − X1) across temperature and pressure. Red regions (Y1 > X1) indicate the self-limiting reaction is energetically preferred (lower ΔG), while green regions (Y1 < X1) indicate continuous etching is preferred. The vertical boundary at 360 K confirms that temperature, not pressure, is the “control knob” dictating the transition between ALE and uncontrolled etching.

Figure 3 shows the effect of etch temperature on the etch per cycle of TiN films for the NbF₅–CCl₄ binary process and the CCl₄-only process. The etch per cycle increases with temperature for both processes, with the binary process consistently exhibiting higher etch rates. No significant etching occurs below 370 °C, and the trend is approximately linear up to 460 °C.

Figure 4 shows the change in film thickness with the total number of etch cycles at 460 °C for TiN, SiO₂, Al₂O₃, and Si₃N₄ films. The TiN film is selectively etched by the CCl₄-only CVE process, with thickness decreasing linearly at approximately 0.3 Å per cycle, reaching about 13 nm after 500 cycles. In contrast, the thickness of Al₂O₃, SiO₂, and Si₃N₄ remains unchanged even after 1000 etch cycles, demonstrating the high selectivity of TiN etching over other materials.
BF-TEM cross-section of pristine SiO₂ fin structures coated with a ~3.3 nm TiN layer, showing high conformality.
BF-TEM cross-section after partial etching (~0.8 nm) of the TiN layer using NbF₅–CCl₄ at 460 °C, showing minimal surface defects.
BF-TEM cross-section after complete removal of the TiN film, showing intact SiO₂ fins and confirming etch selectivity.

Figure 7 presents an XPS depth profile of a partially etched TiN film after 300 cycles of the NbF₅–CCl₄ etch process at 460 °C, with 0.5 s pulses and 6 s N₂ purges. The surface initially contains high carbon (~26%) and oxygen (~31%), along with nitrogen (~20%), titanium (~15%), niobium (~5%), and small amounts of fluorine and silicon (<2%). After 15 s of argon sputtering, carbon decreases to ~4% and further to ~3% after 60 s, while Ti, N, and O stabilize at ~24–32%. Nb and F remain at 5–6% and 0.9–1.7%, respectively, indicating partial incorporation or diffusion within the TiN surface layer. Si originates from the underlying SiO₂. The profile confirms the elemental distribution and partial etch depth of the TiN film.

The figure illustrates how the etch per cycle of TiN films varies with NbF₅ pulse time during the NbF₅–CCl₄ etch process at 460 °C. The etch per cycle increases from about 0.3 Å without NbF₅ to approximately 0.8 Å at a 3 s NbF₅ pulse, showing a gradual increase without clear saturation under the applied conditions.

Figure 2 illustrates the etch per cycle (EPC) of TiN films as a function of CCl₄ pulse time at 460 °C, comparing two etching processes: the NbF₅–CCl₄ binary process and the CCl₄-only CVE process. The EPC increases with CCl₄ pulse time for both processes, with the binary process showing higher etch rates (up to ~0.8 Å at 3 s) compared to the CVE process (~0.5 Å at 3 s). No clear saturation of EPC is observed for either process, and no etching occurs when only NbF₅ is applied, consistent with computational predictions.

Figure 5 shows an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey scan of the SiO₂ surface after complete removal of the TiN film by the CCl₄-based CVE process at 460 °C. The surface is primarily composed of silicon and oxygen, with small amounts of carbon (~5.1 at%) and nitrogen (~1.2 at%). Titanium and chlorine are below the detection limit (<0.1 at%). The nitrogen and carbon signals likely arise from surface contamination.
